Hip Osteoarthritis, Right Unilateral primary osteoarthritis, right hip (M16.11) Plan: Counseling - Hip DJD. Musculoskeletal care: I advised the patient that most patients with hip DJD can achieve some measure of pain relief from nonsurgical options like lifestyle modification, exercise and physical therapy, and use of supportive devices. The importance of maintaining an ideal body weight to reduce stress on the hip joint was emphasized. Expectations: I counseled the patient about the natural history of DJD which typically has exacerbations and remissions. Many people report that changes in the weather also affect the degree of pain from arthritis. For some, conservative management is not satisfactory. These individuals should consider surgical options. Contact Office if your hip arthritis pain worsens, or if the hip becomes warm or accompanied by a fever. Medication Counseling NSAIDS : I discussed with the patient that NSAIDs should be taken with food. Prolonged use of NSAIDs can result in the development of stomach ulcers or bleeding. Patient advised to stop taking NSAIDs if abdominal pain occurs. The patient verbalized understanding of the proper use and possible adverse effects of NSAIDs. All of the patient's questions and concerns were addressed. OTC Recommendations: NSAIDs: Meloxicam to be taken with food. Active Therapy : Active therapies include stretching exercises, weight training (quadriceps strengthening), and cardiovascular conditioning. Passive Therapy : Passive therapies include ice, heat, massage, ultrasound, and electrical stimulation. Your therapist will decide which of these interventions will be most beneficial to you. Surgical Options and Alternatives Intraarticular steroid injection : I discussed with the patient that this involves the injection of a potent antiinflammatory substance (cortisone) into the hip joint. Risks include whitening of the skin at the injection site and a transient rise in blood glucose. Complications are extremely rare; infection is the most common. There is no rule as to how many Russell T Nevins, MD (Primary Provider) (Bill Under) Desert Inn Page 2 (702) 731-1616 Work 2800 East Desert Inn Road (702) 734-4900 Fax Ste 100 Las Vegas, NV 89121-3609 * Auth (Verified) * DESERT DOC ORTHOPAEDIC CENTER Senglaup, Karen Experience. Excellence. Visit Note - January 27, 2026 PMS ID: Sex: DOB: Phone: MRN: 114700PAT000101850 Female 05/03/1955 (716) 868-0626 MM0000101295 injections can be given. Because some research has shown that too much cortisone can damage cartilage, most physicians limit the number of shots they will give you. Total hip replacement : I explained to the patient that this is an option for patients that have severe pain in their hip from arthritis who have failed conservative management. The diseased hip socket and femoral head (the ball) are replaced with a combination of either metal, plastic, ceramic, or a combination of these. The vast majority of patients who undergo this surgery have a significant reduction in pain and a dramatic improvement in their ability to do common activities of daily living. High impact activities should be avoided after surgery. Your surgeon might also have you avoid specific positions that might put you at risk for a dislocation. Risks include but are not limited to infection, injury to blood vessels and nerves, blood clots, fracture, dislocation, leg length discrepancy, and cardiopulmonary complications. Diagnostic Imaging Results I reviewed and discussed the results of the X-Ray(s) with the patient. After discussing the risks, benefits and alternatives, we decided on the following plan for the RIGHT: TOTAL HIP REPLACEMENT and THE PATIENT WAS GIVEN. HANDOUT ABOUT TOTAL HIP REPLACEMENT WITH ALL THE RISKS OF PROCEEDING WITH SURGERY. Plan: Additional Notes. Risks, benefits and alternatives were discussed with the patient including the risks of blood clot, infection, stroke, heart attack, death, disability, loss of range of motion, stiffness, skin healing problems, nerve damage, blood vessel damage, bleeding, leg length discrepancy, fractures, tendon damage, dislocation, persistent pain, swelling, amputation, continued pain requiring further surgery. No guarantees were given and/or implied for complete pain relief. Patient understand all the risks, benefits, and alternatives to surgical intervention. Associated with: Order Plain X-ray/Interpretation Follow up for: Pre-op visit. Other Instructions: Schedule pre-op visit with Viet Nguyen PA-C after all medical clearance has been completed for Right total hip replacement Note: The patient comes in today for evaluation of right hip pain. She states 10 years ago she had increasing right groin pain for about 6 months which later resided. The pain has returned over the past 2 years and has to use a cane for ambulation. She describes the pain as being in the right groin, thigh and radiating down the leg. She states Dr Winder is treating her for bilateral knee osteoarthritis The patient has severe end stage Right hip osteoarthritis On physical examination, the patient is almost auto fused. Treatment options discussed are to continue with behavior modification, intra articular hip injection every 6 months, physical therapy, Non steroidal anti-inflammatories, and Total Hip replacement I also had a lengthy conversation with the patient and her sister that she is at higher risk of falls due to her fused hips and osteoarthritis of bilateral knee. I have recommended she consider a Life Alert. I have also discussed the patient is at higher risk of a leg length discrepancy following total hip replacement due to auto fused right hip with contractures making it difficult to measure leg lengths The patient has failed conservative treatement Russell T Nevins, MD (Primary Provider) (Bill Under) Desert Inn Page 3 (702) 731-1616 Work 2800 East Desert Inn Road (702) 734-4900 Fax Ste 100 Las Vegas, NV 89121-3609 * Auth (Verified) * DESERT DOC ORTHOPAEDIC CENTER Senglaup, Karen Experience. Hip Osteoarthritis, Right Unilateral primary osteoarthritis, right hip (M16.11) Plan: Counseling - Hip DJD. Musculoskeletal care: I advised the patient that most patients with hip DJD can achieve some measure of pain relief from nonsurgical options like lifestyle modification, exercise and physical therapy, and use of supportive devices. The importance of maintaining an ideal body weight to reduce stress on the hip joint was emphasized. Expectations: I counseled the patient about the natural history of DJD which typically has exacerbations and remissions. Many people report that changes in the weather also affect the degree of pain from arthritis. For some, conservative management is not satisfactory. These individuals should consider surgical options. Contact Office if your hip arthritis pain worsens, or if the hip becomes warm or accompanied by a fever. Medication Counseling NSAIDS : I discussed with the patient that NSAIDs should be taken with food. Prolonged use of NSAIDs can result in the development of stomach ulcers or bleeding. Patient advised to stop taking NSAIDs if abdominal pain occurs. The patient verbalized understanding of the proper use and possible adverse effects of NSAIDs. All of the patient's questions and concerns were addressed. OTC Recommendations: NSAIDs: Meloxicam to be taken with food. Active Therapy : Active therapies include stretching exercises, weight training (quadriceps strengthening), and cardiovascular conditioning. Passive Therapy : Passive therapies include ice, heat, massage, ultrasound, and electrical stimulation. Your therapist will decide which of these interventions will be most beneficial to you. Surgical Options and Alternatives Intraarticular steroid injection : I discussed with the patient that this involves the injection of a potent antiinflammatory substance (cortisone) into the hip joint. Risks include whitening of the skin at the injection site and a transient rise in blood glucose. Complications are extremely rare; infection is the most common. Because some research has shown that too much cortisone can damage cartilage, most physicians limit the number of shots they will give you. Total hip replacement : I explained to the patient that this is an option for patients that have severe pain in their hip from arthritis who have failed conservative management. The diseased hip socket and femoral head (the ball) are replaced with a combination of either metal, plastic, ceramic, or a combination of these. The vast majority of patients who undergo this surgery have a significant reduction in pain and a dramatic improvement in their ability to do common activities of daily living. High impact activities should be avoided after surgery. Your surgeon might also have you avoid specific positions that might put you at risk for a dislocation. Risks include but are not limited to infection, injury to blood vessels and nerves, blood clots, fracture, dislocation, leg length discrepancy, and cardiopulmonary complications.
